title: Vertical Datum Reference
abstract: Reusable schema component encoding the vertical datum metadata required for unambiguous height referencing across land and marine domains, as mandated by FMSDI Best Practice 1 (Unified Geospatial Reference). Captures datum name, reference epoch, CRS identifier, separation surface model, transformation method, vertical orientation convention, and vertical accuracy.
description:
The vertical datum is the single most consequential piece of metadata for any elevation or depth observation in the coastal zone. The OGC Federated Marine SDI 2024 Pilot (FMSDI-2024, document 24-064) identified disconnected vertical and horizontal datums between topographic and hydrographic datasets as 'one of the most persistent technical barriers to land-sea integration'.

The Compusult demonstrator in the FMSDI-2024 pilot encountered this problem directly: early iterations of their vessel navigation process assumed Ordnance Datum Newlyn (ODN, orientation=up) for tidal data that was in fact referenced to Southampton Chart Datum (orientation=down, offset -2.74m relative to ODN). This caused water depths to display incorrectly until the correct datum was applied.

The Pangaea demonstrator similarly identified that 'inconsistent or missing vertical datum metadata remains a major barrier to automation' in their DGGS-based integration approach.

This building block provides a standard component that any feature type carrying elevation, depth, or height values can embed to ensure full vertical datum provenance.

## Common Datums Used in Intertidal Zone Work

| Datum | Abbreviation | Orientation | EPSG | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chart Datum | CD | down | 5861 | UK/IHO navigational charts |
| Lowest Astronomical Tide | LAT | down | — | IHO standard chart datum |
| Mean Sea Level | MSL | up | 5714 | General geodetic reference |
| Mean High Water Springs | MHWS | up | — | Jurisdictional / legal shoreline |
| Ordnance Datum Newlyn | ODN | up | 5701 | UK national land levelling |
| WGS84 Ellipsoid | WGS84 | up | 4979 | GNSS, satellite imagery |
| Southampton Chart Datum | SCD | down | — | The Solent, offset -2.74m from ODN |

## Vertical Separation Surfaces Hierarchy

Transformations between datums follow this hierarchy from geometric to tidal:

```
Ellipsoid (WGS84/G2139, ETRS89)
   └─► Geoid (EGM2008, OSGM15)
        └─► Mean Tidal Surface (MSL, MHWS, MLW)
             └─► Chart Datum (CD, LAT)
```

National models implement this hierarchy:
- NOAA VDatum 4.3 (United States)
- UKHO VORF 2022 (United Kingdom)
- CHS HyVSEPs (Canada)

FMSDI BP1 mandates the use of these national models where they exist.

## Usage Guidance

1. Include this object as a nested property (`verticalDatum`) in any GeoJSON feature or observation schema that carries elevation, depth, or height values.
2. Always set `verticalOrientation`. Do not assume it from context — the same positive numerical value means higher elevation under 'up' (land/topographic convention) but greater depth under 'down' (hydrographic convention). This is the most frequent source of integration errors.
3. Always record `transformationMethod` when datum conversion has been applied. Its absence implies either no conversion was performed or provenance is unknown.
4. When `separationSurface.type` is 'tidal', always specify `modelReference` (e.g. 'UKHO VORF 2022') to support reproducibility.
5. Before merging land elevation grids (orientation=up) with bathymetric grids (orientation=down), convert both to a common orientation and datum. Positive depths (down convention) must be negated before arithmetic comparison with positive heights (up convention).
6. Record `datumEpoch` whenever using a dynamic reference frame subject to secular change (e.g. tide gauge benchmark migration, vertical land motion).

usageGuidance.step1: Embed a `verticalDatum` property of this type in every feature schema that includes elevation, depth, or height.
usageGuidance.step2: Always set `verticalOrientation` to 'up' (land/topographic) or 'down' (hydrographic/bathymetric). This single field prevents the most common integration error.
usageGuidance.step3: Populate `crsIdentifier` with the EPSG URI from http://www.opengis.net/def/crs/EPSG/0/{code}. Examples: EPSG:5714 (MSL height), EPSG:5861 (LAT depth), EPSG:7405 (ODN height), EPSG:4979 (WGS84 3D).
usageGuidance.step4: Record `transformationMethod` whenever a datum conversion was applied. For UK coastal work this is typically 'UKHO VORF 2022'; for US work 'NOAA VDatum 4.3'.
usageGuidance.step5: Quantify `verticalAccuracy` using IHO S-44 confidence levels for hydrographic surveys or ISO 19115 DQ_PositionalAccuracy for topographic data.
usageGuidance.step6: When aggregating data from multiple sources, normalize to a single `verticalOrientation` before computing differences or generating composite products.

references.title: FMSDI Best Practice 1 (Unified Geospatial Reference) — vertical separation surfaces and transformation models
Compusult D100 — Vertical datum consistency lesson learned (ODN vs Southampton Chart Datum -2.74m)
Pangaea D100 — Inconsistent vertical datum metadata as automation barrier
IHO S-100 Universal Hydrographic Data Model
